title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 contract for recycled asphalt crushing to King Gravel, and directed staff to develop enforcement language for sewer discharge violations at $100/day. The committee also approved construction service proposals for two road projects and discussed flooding mitigation efforts and upcoming infrastructure work.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Reports
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
Construction Service Proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue road repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for settlement issues over sanitary sewer trenches, and upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street for manhole and pavement issues.

Staff reported on seasonal activities including removal of road limits, mailbox and sign repairs, and spring curb line sweeping. Significant discussion focused on flooding issues near Weston Avenue requiring dewatering pumps for approximately a week. Staff is exploring permanent pump solutions. The committee discussed the need for better drainage solutions in the Zenzer area where groundwater issues persist. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was also detailed.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and ongoing work at the treatment plant for well 3 addition. Significant discussion focused on clear water infiltration into the sanitary system - in April, 37 million gallons entered the water system but 53-54 million gallons were treated at the sewer plant, indicating roughly 15 million gallons of infiltration. Staff identified sump pumps illegally connected to sanitary sewers as a major contributor. The committee discussed the upcoming I&I study with in-pipe technologies to identify problem areas.

discussions.item: Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Reports
discussions.body: Michael presented the annual PSC report showing operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was reported at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million, up 6.7% due to infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8 and water main from the Weston Avenue project. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers across Weston (5,250), Rothschild (830), and a few in Schofield. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. While the village has been successful obtaining grants for one-time projects, recurring funding for street maintenance and snow plowing remains inadequate, requiring subsidization from general tax dollars. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed this practice saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, translating to about $130,000 in savings across the three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of illegal sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members distinguished between unknowing violations (correctable with notice) and willful violations deserving higher penalties. Trustee Zagami suggested waiting for the I&I study results to identify problem areas, then sending notices with a compliance deadline. The committee agreed $100/day was appropriate for initial enforcement. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language not exceeding $100/day passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 of Radkey Avenue where water/sewer mains currently dead-end. Rather than requiring the builder to install mains through outlot one, staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. The committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029, at which time the owner would pay assessment for main extension. Motion passed unanimously with the 2029 deadline amendment.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for the Military Road project from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction and is responsible for street/storm water items; Weston is responsible for water and sewer items totaling an estimated $94,860. Water main relocation is needed due to conflict with storm sewer improvements. Motion to recommend board approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented construction inspection service proposals received late Friday. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730, but MSA planned to use a summer intern while Clark Dietz would use full-time staff. Staff recommended Clark Dietz for the extra assurance despite the slightly higher cost. Motion to approve MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day maximum
Approve agreement with Radkey Avenue property owner for temporary utility connection with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project ($73,730)
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration for next meeting
Staff to provide update on street lighting for future meeting
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations, reports, old business
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by motion, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as secretary.
Minutes from April 21st were approved unanimously, seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the event, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year and requested better tracking of room usage from hotels. A commissioner requested room data be included in future reports. The commission approved a $5,000 grant (same as last year) unanimously, with plans to evaluate the impact of no Marshfield competition for next year's consideration.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates and noted that Anastasia won the competition. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, and this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing technical details about gas pipeline easements, future building expansion, and environmental protection measures.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff comments

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated 90 degrees and moved north to accommodate a 36-inch gas pipeline easement running under the parking lot. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth, up from current staffing of 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. Discussion included an alternate storage shed location, with commissioners recommending it be placed further north to allow for future building expansion. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code requirements. Motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter inspection focusing on material handling and containment. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg Avenue has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and predates the ordinance. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring that facility into compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint plan commission/village board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites to commissioners.

Electric vehicle charging stations were noted for a future fall agenda. Commissioner inquired about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission, potentially as part of the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video call. He requested the commission urge staff to waive excessive landscaping requirements for the new police department project similar to accommodations made for the Greenheck Turner building, noting taxpayers will bear the costs. He also asked that the commission request annual reports on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, expressing concern that some zoning complaints go unresolved for years.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with semi-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to contact existing salt storage facility near Sternberg Avenue about wellhead protection compliance
Joint steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites and information about residential TIF presentation to commissioners

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No formal votes were taken beyond approval of minutes. The meeting provided an overview of services, budgets, and ongoing initiatives including homelessness response efforts and veteran services.

agenda.item: Call to order and confirmation of remote participation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Educational presentations introduction and review of standing committee ordinances
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under section 2.04 subsection 5, including oversight of Veteran Service, Social Services, and Health departments, plus affiliated entities North Central Health Care and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He described the committee's relationship with subordinate boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, Board of Health, and North Central Community Action Program.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200 licenses overseen, the Nurse Family Partnership program with its first graduates, a new homeless system response position, and public health vending machines offering free tick kits. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE across four divisions. Administrator Leonard noted about half of the department's $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant funding from public charges for services and intergovernmental grants.

Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness following news of an H5 virus outbreak on a cruise ship. Suginaka confirmed the department has plans in place, has conducted measles response training exercises, and staff are trained in emergency response from COVID experience. She explained the department would reallocate staff if needed and noted they monitor foodborne illness outbreaks, STIs in the jail population, and receive alerts about potential disease contacts.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, describing the department's 140 FTE staff working in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, noting significant numbers of clients had never had credit accounts, bank accounts, or formal leases. The department's $22 million budget includes about $7 million in tax levy.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the office grew from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and had 8,149 client interactions, up from 5,900. He noted $44 million in annual VA compensation flows to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo discussed housing stability work with Center for Veterans Issues, suicide prevention initiatives (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and new legislation helping National Guard and Reserve veterans. Each staff member averages 20-30 contacts daily.

Executive Director Mike Ray described the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He explained services including the Meals on Wheels nutrition program, Medicare/Medicaid assistance, falls prevention, dementia care, and serving as the entry point for Family Care and IRIS programs. Ray noted significant financial challenges as federal funding has remained flat while nutrition costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing reductions in meals served and staff. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present to full county board with 2025 annual report data
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to future meetings
Next meeting corrected to Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM
Committee members invited to submit agenda items via email to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bicycle regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call, and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, e-bicycles
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 per single family unit to $761, aligned with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed cost data for park maintenance, calling the decision 'irresponsible.' Trustee Barb supported the fee increase noting population growth requires more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 also passed unanimously with motion by Herming, second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional fire inspection reciprocity agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover Belt, and Riverside. Once a food truck is inspected by any participating municipality, a sticker indicates compliance for all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously with motion by Lope, second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the policy, with some trustees wanting to remove language requiring health, safety, or welfare reasons for remote attendance. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy by striking sentences requiring justification, arguing chairs shouldn't determine whose excuse is 'good enough.' The amendment passed with two opposing votes from Trustees Herling and Lobsra. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The board approved a secondhand article dealer and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street, with an amendment requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Motion by Herling, second by Clark, passed unanimously as amended.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new Kennedy Park location after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue site to development. Trustee Lisa Beck raised safety concerns about vehicles parking on grass. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor stalls and vendors appreciate having their vehicles with them. Discussion included whether to potentially discontinue the market if a suitable location cannot be found. Renee clarified the market is not losing money and detailed grant funding for SNAP/EBT token programs. This was discussion only with no action taken.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Herling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. The board agreed to include committee applications in future board packets.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioning administrator accountability. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed. He called for more transparent public discussions and criticized lack of information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel again spoke questioning the justification for fee amounts without detailed park maintenance cost data.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective immediately per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor regional inspection reciprocity ordinance adopted
E-bicycle regulations ordinance adopted
Nuisances ordinance amended
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC license approved pending background check and occupancy permit
Resolution supporting long-term sustainable transportation funding adopted
Comprehensive Plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Speed limit study on Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board
Committee application materials to be included in future board packets
Intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspections to be drafted

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the at-large Executive Committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton following the April severe weather emergency. The board heard public comment from affected Ringle residents requesting further fee waivers.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments by County Board Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, mentoring new supervisors, and noting 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Paper ballots were cast, and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton for disaster-related debris following severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. The state is waiving $13.10 and Ringle is waiving $2.33 of fees. Leonard noted the county will not meet the $670,743 damage threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle to qualify. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive all fees and extend the timeline beyond 60 days. Multiple supervisors discussed concerns about timing, insurance delays, and future policy. The board unanimously voted to suspend rules to hear the resolution, then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26.
Items C10 through C14A were approved unanimously on a motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to address the board regarding the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive all fees for disaster debris for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle have waived their portions and emphasizing the community has been self-reliant with limited county financial assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for underinsured residents and noting some insurance adjusters have not yet arrived, asking for more than 60 days of relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved: Landfill tipping fees reduced to approximately $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with community foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid waste management board directed to develop clear policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future
Resolution 24-26 approved: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved: Social services budget amendment

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and advanced a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-affected properties. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
Department presentations - Parks, Recreation and Forestry overview
Department presentations - Conservation, Planning and Zoning overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval; Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make these conforming uses rather than non-conforming. No natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve; Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver consideration for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-damaged properties, noting the governor's emergency declaration. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individual disaster victims not in declared areas. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup costs. After discussion about tracking costs and precedent, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or pouts fees. Supervisor K seconded the amendment. The amendment passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview of the department's operations, explaining the landfill serves as a regional facility for Marathon, Portage, and Shawano counties. He discussed the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032, and upcoming capital projects totaling millions of dollars. Marilyn Bend praised the department for keeping private landfill costs in line.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. He explained the department operates both county and city park systems with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service. The committee thanked him for his contributions.

actionItems: Rezoning approved for James Offer and David Tract property in Town of Green Valley - forwarded to County Board
Rezoning approved for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property in Town of McMillan - forwarded to County Board
Modified fee waiver resolution (limited to septic reconnect fees only) forwarded to County Board for tornado-impacted properties
Staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy consideration
Conservation Planning and Zoning to present summer educational meeting on conservation topics

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved the installation of new kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River and deferred discussion on dogs at the aquatic center until next month. The meeting featured extensive discussion about Kennedy Park fundraising progress and timeline, with public commenters expressing concerns about meeting times and financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting were approved with a motion, second, and unanimous voice vote with no discussion.

Sean Osterbrink presented a timeline document tracking Kennedy Park's development from the comprehensive outdoor recreation plan approval in November 2023 through the recent ribbon cutting on May 9th. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about funding structure, confirming that village capital dollars work in conjunction with Friends of Kennedy Park fundraising. Osterbrink noted Friends of Kennedy Park currently has approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights, and plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. The chair emphasized that Friends of Kennedy Park is a volunteer organization deserving grace and that master plans are guides subject to change.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos showing fallen trees in the river that need removal and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during logging operations. The mats were placed with TC Energy oversight. Dan explained the trees were removed due to high water levels that would make the river impassable as water drops.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Committee Chair Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy who attended specifically to oppose the idea (stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.') had his objection noted and was invited to return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Discussion focused on challenges removing graffiti from concrete benches and garbage cans along the trail. Osterbrink explained their sandblaster pressure washer cannot access the trail due to bridge width limitations and soft ground conditions. Committee members suggested anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, or organizing volunteer work days. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan Rau from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed kayak launch signs for installation at three Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs include maps, river gauge QR codes, and location-specific history panels. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants at no cost to the village. Committee approved installation of the signs unanimously by voice vote to be forwarded to the village board.

Staff raised potential discussion of food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving a request from a community member. Committee agreed to add this as a future agenda item along with the deferred dog day discussion. Osterbrink announced the Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler next Tuesday.

publicComment: Two public comments were received. In person, Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals don't belong in public pools. Online, Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston) criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial transparency, specifically the $8,700 figure for baseball equipment and asking about the location of the $10,000 grant funds and glow event proceeds.
actionItems: Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks - forwarded to village board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to explore graffiti removal and cleaning options for Eau Claire River Trail
Food allergy awareness signage to be added as future agenda topic
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held a routine meeting featuring approval of previous minutes, acknowledgment of April budget and TIF reports, and an educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam about the clerk's office operations including elections, licensing, and tax collection. Committee member Evan Daniels announced he is stepping down from the committee due to moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, and staff remarks

discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: April budget status report and TIF reports
discussions.body: Jessica provided information on TIF district closure timeline, indicating bonds are due October 1st and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. A committee member asked if final numbers still look good, and staff confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items 5, 6, and 7 made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Municipal Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations. She explained they now have two full-time employees and one part-time employee (Leah), down from four full-time staff before her tenure. The presentation covered election administration including Badger Books (electronic poll books costing about $11,000 for four units), licensing (51 total liquor licenses), tax collection (84% of $22 million collected at the clerk's office counter), and pet licensing brought back in-house saving roughly $5,495 annually. Discussion included why postage jumped from $6,000 to $15,000 (due to fall presidential election absentee ballots), the increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County requirements, and newsletter costs of approximately $30,000 annually for the quarterly 'Wire' publication sent to 5,089 postal patrons.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at the new location has been going well, with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. She has been present for 6-7 hours each Saturday since opening. Committee members discussed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff confirming only the west side of Alderson allows parking now. One vendor reported being 'very happy' with the setup and location.

Evan Daniels announced this would be his last meeting as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. The chair thanked him for his service and noted the committee now has open spots to fill.

actionItems: CDA resolution to call TIF bonds expected this summer, with bonds due October 1st
Staff to continue working with Ellers and Wisconsin Associated Trust for bond closure process
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots including Evan Daniels' vacancy
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, approving amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, recommending an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorizing a raze order for an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request from Mount Olive Church.

agenda.item: Call to order and pledge of allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location, stating it was 'a terrible execution' and predicting grass damage. He questioned ADA-compliant handicap parking placement on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and raised concerns about code enforcement cases that have been pending for extended periods without visible progress.

Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement role after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired up to 40 officers with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including switching tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) and adding language about compliance with jurisdictional authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extended discussion on proposed e-bike regulations. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee member Mike Olsen raised concerns about electric scooters used by middle schoolers. Chief Hunt suggested adding 'with exemption to kids play scooters' for officer discretion. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property, seconded by Sherwanka, passed unanimously.

Mike Olsen presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm, October 31-May 1) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (typically 5 hours or less). Michael Wodalski from BPW suggested either eliminating overnight parking restrictions entirely or signing specific problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed elimination, noting overnight restrictions give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. Committee decided to table the item until July for staff to identify specific problem blocks requiring signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk on Alderson Street between the church and DC Everest school parking. Michael Wodalski estimated $2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for lighted system. Committee agreed the church should pay for installation. Motion by Olsen that if Mount Olive covers the cost, the committee recommends board approval, seconded by John Sherwanka, passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. One inspection provides a sticker valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Chief noted a recent inspection found significant safety violations including improper LP piping. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors requiring correction for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue with out-of-sequence apartment complex. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with nine separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend corrections to Board of Trustees.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011, was posted in 2021, and a planned fire department training burn was cancelled due to unsafe conditions. Staff noted chickens were previously housed inside. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer this route. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt provided extensive public comment criticizing the new farmers market location execution, questioning ADA compliance of handicap parking on grass, asking about the origin of food truck fire inspections, raising concerns about food truck permitting on public property, and questioning accountability on long-pending code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to Board of Trustees
Staff to develop specific problem blocks for overnight parking signage by July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding crosswalk cost-sharing
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 (mobile food vendor fire inspections) to Board
Recommend address corrections for Delicoski, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue properties to Board
Begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
